High BaSr granitoids are conventionally interpreted as products of metasomatized mantle sources, yet whether such signatures can instead arise from crustal recycling remains a contentious and underexplored alternative. Here we test this hypothesis using a suite of felsic dykes coeval with the world-class gold mineralization of the Jiaodong Peninsula, eastern North China Craton. The dykes exhibit shoshonitic affinities and pronounced BaSr enrichment (Ba = 2333–2747 ppm; Sr = 461–1050 ppm). Crucially, zircon UPb geochronology constrains their emplacement to 120–115 Ma—synchronous with the peak gold event at ca. 120 Ma—while in situ Hf isotopes yield strongly negative εHf(t) values (−25 to −17) and Neoarchean-Paleoproterozoic two-stage model ages (TDM 2 = 2.01–2.73 Ga). These data strongly indicate a source dominated by remelting of ancient continental crust, compelling evidence for a dominant ancient crustal source. The elevated BaSr concentrations, shown to be largely primary rather than alteration-induced, are consistent with inheritance from this recycled crustal protolith. These results demonstrate that high BaSr signatures in post-Archean settings do not require a mantle origin but can arise through crustal reworking. The dykes record crustal melting triggered by lithospheric destruction and are spatially, temporally, and petrogenetically associated with the coeval gold-forming system, highlighting their significance as integral components of the regional metallogenic event.

item.title: Geodynamic Controls on Magmatic Ni-Cu-Platinum Group Element Sulfide Mineral Systems Through Time
item.dc:creator: David A. Holwell
Daryl E. Blanks
Marco Fiorentini
Erin S. Thompson
Isra Ezad
Stephen Foley
Weronika Gorczyk
Jason Bennett
Nicole Januszczak
Elizabeth Sharman
Natalie Caciagli
Lisa Hart-Madigan
Steven Rennick
item.description: Abstract Most models for the formation of magmatic Ni-Cu-platinum group element (PGE) sulfide deposits invoke high-temperature events (e.g., plumes) to sufficiently melt enough peridotite mantle to produce MgO- and Ni-Cu-PGE–rich magmas, and the deposits are thus intrinsically linked to the emplacement of large igneous provinces (LIPs) in mostly extensional tectonic regimes. However, many Ni-Cu deposits are hosted by intrusions unrelated to LIP events, having formed during compressional orogenic events. We present a new conceptual framework for global magmatic sulfide deposits based on chalcophile element geochemistry, which reflects distinct combinations of mantle source composition (anhydrous peridotite vs. hydrous peridotite/pyroxenite) and the temperature and/or volume of melting. Low volumes of melting of hydrous mantle at low temperatures (<1,200°C) produce alkalic ultramafic-hosted, Ni-poor, Cu-Au–dominant, low-temperature Cu (LT-Cu) deposits, displaying relative enrichment in Cu, Au, and Te over the other chalcophile elements. Greater volumes of melting of a hydrous source produce mafic-ultramafic-hosted low-temperature Ni (LT-Ni) deposits defined by distinctly PGE-poor chemistries. High-temperature (>1,200°C) melting of more anhydrous mantle peridotite forms both mafic-ultramafic-hosted high-temperature Ni (HT-Ni) deposits enriched in all chalcophile elements and layered mafic intrusion-hosted high-temperature PGE (HT-PGE) deposits, which display relative PGE enrichment. Very-high-temperature melting of peridotite mantle forms komatiite-hosted very-high-temperature Ni (VHT-Ni) deposits with chalcophile element profiles close to primitive mantle ratios. Temporally and spatially, all VHT and HT deposits are inextricably linked to LIPs throughout Earth’s history. The major HT-PGE deposits are all >2.0 Ga and hosted within cratons, the VHT-Ni deposits are all >1.8 Ga and hosted in komatiites, and the HT-Ni deposits are distributed throughout geologic time and are mostly located at cratonic margins. Conversely, LT deposits are all younger than 2.0 Ga, are almost never associated with LIPs, and have a more varied spatial distribution, but are more commonly located within orogenic belts. These secular changes in the nature of magmatic sulfide systems through time are a function of (1) a cooling mantle from the Archean to the present, (2) varying compositions of mantle as a product of increased mantle metasomatic processes through time, (3) the mineralogical deportment of chalcophile metals in mantle rocks, and (4) the variety of thermal triggers to melt such assemblages. We present a revised mineral system framework for the source and geodynamic controls on magmatic sulfides that takes into account all these criteria and allows enhanced targeting capability and prediction of the metal fertility of potential deposits. Published: 2026-06-18T17:37:11+00:00 Venue: Economic Geology Score: 0.635 (must_read)

Abstract Most models for the formation of magmatic Ni-Cu-platinum group element (PGE) sulfide deposits invoke high-temperature events (e.g., plumes) to sufficiently melt enough peridotite mantle to produce MgO- and Ni-Cu-PGE–rich magmas, and the deposits are thus intrinsically linked to the emplacement of large igneous provinces (LIPs) in mostly extensional tectonic regimes. We present a new conceptual framework for global magmatic sulfide deposits based on chalcophile element geochemistry, which reflects distinct combinations of mantle source composition (anhydrous peridotite vs. hydrous peridotite/pyroxenite) and the temperature and/or volume of melting. Greater volumes of melting of a hydrous source produce mafic-ultramafic-hosted low-temperature Ni (LT-Ni) deposits defined by distinctly PGE-poor chemistries. High-temperature (>1,200°C) melting of more anhydrous mantle peridotite forms both mafic-ultramafic-hosted high-temperature Ni (HT-Ni) deposits enriched in all chalcophile elements and layered mafic intrusion-hosted high-temperature PGE (HT-PGE) deposits, which display relative PGE enrichment. Very-high-temperature melting of peridotite mantle forms komatiite-hosted very-high-temperature Ni (VHT-Ni) deposits with chalcophile element profiles close to primitive mantle ratios. We present a revised mineral system framework for the source and geodynamic controls on magmatic sulfides that takes into account all these criteria and allows enhanced targeting capability and prediction of the metal fertility of potential deposits.

item.title: Slab flattening in the mantle transition zone drives extrusion and upwelling of ancient subducted material
item.dc:creator: Liang Zhou
Hong-Yan Li
Bo Pan
Zhong-Hai Li
Yangfan Deng
Shui-Jiong Wang
Chun Yang
Yi-Gang Xu
item.description: Global seismic tomography reveals that many subducted slabs flatten and stagnate within the mantle transition zone (MTZ), particularly beneath the western Pacific, yet how this process influences mantle material redistribution and overlying intraplate volcanism remains a fundamental question. We address this issue using integrated 40Ar/39Ar geochronology, whole-rock and mineral chemistry, and Sr–Nd–Pb–Hf isotopic analyses of basalts from the Changbaishan volcanic field (NE China), a long-lived volcanic system situated above the stagnant Pacific slab. Our results document a systematic temporal evolution over ∼22 million years. The earliest low-Ti basalts (∼22.4 Ma; 1.1–1.3 wt.% TiO2) display arc-like signatures, high olivine Ca/Al (5–45) and Mn/Fe (>1.7) ratios, and elevated oxygen fugacity (∼∆FMQ+2), indicating derivation from an oxidized, carbonated peridotitic source. Subsequent high-Ti (∼21.3 Ma; 2.4–2.8 wt.% TiO2) and later shield-stage basalts (∼3–1 Ma) exhibit ocean island basalt-like affinities, low olivine Ca/Al (∼5) and Mn/Fe (<1.5), and were derived from a reduced (∼∆FMQ), pyroxenite-bearing mantle source. Isotopically, the earliest basalts record a stronger contribution from recently subducted components, whereas subsequent basalts show progressively greater involvement of an ancient, enriched mantle (EM-1) component. Together with regional isotopic comparisons of Mesozoic–Cenozoic basalts, these data indicate that EM-1-like signatures became prominent mainly after ∼22 Ma in NE China. By integrating these geochemical signatures with geophysical observations, numerical modeling, and plate reconstruction evidence, we propose that Pacific slab rollback and flattening since ∼22 Ma drove upwelling of MTZ material containing resident EM-1 components. Fluids from the stagnant slab and destabilized hydrous MTZ minerals reduced mantle density, enabling buoyant ascent and decompression melting that generated the voluminous shield-stage magmas. Our findings support a close genetic linkage among slab flattening, dynamic source contributions from the MTZ, and intraplate volcanism, highlighting the MTZ as a crucial geochemical reservoir that mediates material cycling between Earth’s shallow and deep mantle domains. Published: 2026-06-18T00:38:04+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.629 (must_read)

Authors: Liang Zhou; Hong-Yan Li; Bo Pan; Zhong-Hai Li; Yangfan Deng; Shui-Jiong Wang; Chun Yang; Yi-Gang Xu

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120185

Relevance: 0.629 (must_read)

Global seismic tomography reveals that many subducted slabs flatten and stagnate within the mantle transition zone (MTZ), particularly beneath the western Pacific, yet how this process influences mantle material redistribution and overlying intraplate volcanism remains a fundamental question. We address this issue using integrated 40Ar/39Ar geochronology, whole-rock and mineral chemistry, and Sr–Nd–Pb–Hf isotopic analyses of basalts from the Changbaishan volcanic field (NE China), a long-lived volcanic system situated above the stagnant Pacific slab. Our results document a systematic temporal evolution over ∼22 million years. The earliest low-Ti basalts (∼22.4 Ma; 1.1–1.3 wt.% TiO2) display arc-like signatures, high olivine Ca/Al (5–45) and Mn/Fe (>1.7) ratios, and elevated oxygen fugacity (∼∆FMQ+2), indicating derivation from an oxidized, carbonated peridotitic source. Subsequent high-Ti (∼21.3 Ma; 2.4–2.8 wt.% TiO2) and later shield-stage basalts (∼3–1 Ma) exhibit ocean island basalt-like affinities, low olivine Ca/Al (∼5) and Mn/Fe (<1.5), and were derived from a reduced (∼∆FMQ), pyroxenite-bearing mantle source. Isotopically, the earliest basalts record a stronger contribution from recently subducted components, whereas subsequent basalts show progressively greater involvement of an ancient, enriched mantle (EM-1) component. Together with regional isotopic comparisons of Mesozoic–Cenozoic basalts, these data indicate that EM-1-like signatures became prominent mainly after ∼22 Ma in NE China. By integrating these geochemical signatures with geophysical observations, numerical modeling, and plate reconstruction evidence, we propose that Pacific slab rollback and flattening since ∼22 Ma drove upwelling of MTZ material containing resident EM-1 components. Fluids from the stagnant slab and destabilized hydrous MTZ minerals reduced mantle density, enabling buoyant ascent and decompression melting that generated the voluminous shield-stage magmas. Our findings support a close genetic linkage among slab flattening, dynamic source contributions from the MTZ, and intraplate volcanism, highlighting the MTZ as a crucial geochemical reservoir that mediates material cycling between Earth’s shallow and deep mantle domains.

item.title: Timing of magmatic–hydrothermal activity and gold mineralization at the Jiudian Gold Deposit, Jiaodong Peninsula, Eastern China: Constraints from integrated U–Pb and Rb–Sr geochronology
item.dc:creator: Zuowen Dai
Yuling Xie
Zhendong Cai
Yunwei Qu
Xingyuan Wang
Leilei Dong
Dunfang Qiu
Jun Hu
Zichong Song
item.description: The Jiudian gold deposit, a fault-controlled quartz–sulfide vein system hosted mainly within the Linglong granite, represents one of the largest gold accumulations along the southern segment of the Zhaoping Fault in the Jiaodong gold province, eastern China. However, the timing of gold mineralization and its relationship to local magmatism remain poorly constrained, hindering a comprehensive understanding of the deposit’s metallogenic evolution. To address this issue, we integrate detailed petrography with in-situ geochronological and geochemical analyses, including zircon U–Pb dating of granitic pegmatites, garnet U–Pb geochronology and trace-element analyses of newly recognized barren skarns, and Rb–Sr dating of hydrothermal muscovite and sericite from auriferous quartz veins. Petrographic observations, SEM–EDS imaging, and mineral chemistry reveal two generations of hydrothermal garnet (Grt-1 and Grt-2) within the skarns. Zircon U–Pb analyses of pegmatites yield weighted mean 206Pb/238U ages of 145.9 ± 1.6 Ma and 145.0 ± 1.1 Ma, whereas garnet U–Pb dating produces indistinguishable lower-intercept ages of 145.8 ± 1.2 Ma and 145.0 ± 1.1 Ma for Grt-1 and Grt-2, respectively. These results demonstrate that skarn formation was coeval with pegmatitic magmatism at ∼145 Ma. In contrast, hydrothermal muscovite and sericite intimately associated with electrum, the principal gold-bearing phase, yield Rb–Sr isochron ages of 119.8 ± 8.6 Ma and 119.2 ± 2.7 Ma, constraining the timing of gold mineralization to ∼120 Ma. Combined with previously published geochronological data, our results indicate that the ∼145 Ma skarn-forming event represents a gold-barren magmatic–hydrothermal episode genetically related to emplacement of the Linglong granite, whereas economically significant gold mineralization occurred ∼25 Myr later during an independent hydrothermal event. The ∼120 Ma mineralization age is synchronous with the widespread intrusion of mafic dikes in the Jiudian area and the broader Jiaodong gold province, implying that mantle-derived magmatism likely played a key role in triggering gold mineralization. This study provides robust evidence for the temporal decoupling between early granite-related hydrothermal activity and late gold mineralization in the Jiudian deposit, and further supports a multi-stage metallogenic model involving discrete magmatic-hydrothermal events for the Jiaodong gold province. Published: 2026-06-14T17:58:20+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.628 (consider)

The Jiudian gold deposit, a fault-controlled quartz–sulfide vein system hosted mainly within the Linglong granite, represents one of the largest gold accumulations along the southern segment of the Zhaoping Fault in the Jiaodong gold province, eastern China. However, the timing of gold mineralization and its relationship to local magmatism remain poorly constrained, hindering a comprehensive understanding of the deposit’s metallogenic evolution. To address this issue, we integrate detailed petrography with in-situ geochronological and geochemical analyses, including zircon U–Pb dating of granitic pegmatites, garnet U–Pb geochronology and trace-element analyses of newly recognized barren skarns, and Rb–Sr dating of hydrothermal muscovite and sericite from auriferous quartz veins. Petrographic observations, SEM–EDS imaging, and mineral chemistry reveal two generations of hydrothermal garnet (Grt-1 and Grt-2) within the skarns. Zircon U–Pb analyses of pegmatites yield weighted mean 206Pb/238U ages of 145.9 ± 1.6 Ma and 145.0 ± 1.1 Ma, whereas garnet U–Pb dating produces indistinguishable lower-intercept ages of 145.8 ± 1.2 Ma and 145.0 ± 1.1 Ma for Grt-1 and Grt-2, respectively. These results demonstrate that skarn formation was coeval with pegmatitic magmatism at ∼145 Ma. In contrast, hydrothermal muscovite and sericite intimately associated with electrum, the principal gold-bearing phase, yield Rb–Sr isochron ages of 119.8 ± 8.6 Ma and 119.2 ± 2.7 Ma, constraining the timing of gold mineralization to ∼120 Ma. Combined with previously published geochronological data, our results indicate that the ∼145 Ma skarn-forming event represents a gold-barren magmatic–hydrothermal episode genetically related to emplacement of the Linglong granite, whereas economically significant gold mineralization occurred ∼25 Myr later during an independent hydrothermal event. The ∼120 Ma mineralization age is synchronous with the widespread intrusion of mafic dikes in the Jiudian area and the broader Jiaodong gold province, implying that mantle-derived magmatism likely played a key role in triggering gold mineralization. This study provides robust evidence for the temporal decoupling between early granite-related hydrothermal activity and late gold mineralization in the Jiudian deposit, and further supports a multi-stage metallogenic model involving discrete magmatic-hydrothermal events for the Jiaodong gold province.

item.title: Forearc peridotites record interaction of slab melt with nascent mantle wedge in the southwest Mariana arc
item.dc:creator: Junhua Yao
Guo-Liang Zhang
Ji Zhang
De-Feng He
item.description: Plate subduction plays a critical role in plate tectonics, yet the interaction between the subducted plate and the mantle wedge in nascent subduction zones remains poorly understood. The southwestern Mariana arc located in the western Pacific near Micronesia, representing a modern analog of subduction infancy, provides an ideal setting to investigate subduction infancy and the recycling of subducted materials. Here we present petrographic, mineralogical, and whole-rock geochemical data for serpentinized forearc peridotites recovered from the southwest Mariana arc. These forearc peridotites can be divided into two groups based on their chromite Cr# values. The high-Cr# group (Cr#: 0.78−0.86) records interaction with high-Si boninitic melts, as indicated by the presence of chromite-hosted amphibole inclusions whose trace-element compositions are consistent with a high-Si boninitic affinity, whereas the low-Cr# group (Cr#: 0.49−0.74) is interpreted to have experienced metasomatism by melts of adakitic affinity, as suggested by the occurrence of clinopyroxene with adakite-like geochemical characteristics. Compared to the mature Izu-Bonin and central Mariana arcs, the southwestern Mariana forearc peridotites are geochemically more enriched, showing higher SiO2 and Fe2O3T, lower MgO, and up to an order of magnitude higher heavy rare earth element contents. These features indicate that the forearc peridotites in immature subduction zones preserve more pristine records of early mantle wedge processes, owing to limited subsequent modification after the initial stages of subduction. Our findings challenge the prevailing view that forearc peridotites are highly depleted and refractory and highlight the importance of early melt−rock interaction in shaping sub-arc mantle wedge compositions during subduction initiation. Published: 2026-06-16T22:08:09+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.627 (consider)

Plate subduction plays a critical role in plate tectonics, yet the interaction between the subducted plate and the mantle wedge in nascent subduction zones remains poorly understood. The southwestern Mariana arc located in the western Pacific near Micronesia, representing a modern analog of subduction infancy, provides an ideal setting to investigate subduction infancy and the recycling of subducted materials. Here we present petrographic, mineralogical, and whole-rock geochemical data for serpentinized forearc peridotites recovered from the southwest Mariana arc. These forearc peridotites can be divided into two groups based on their chromite Cr# values. The high-Cr# group (Cr#: 0.78−0.86) records interaction with high-Si boninitic melts, as indicated by the presence of chromite-hosted amphibole inclusions whose trace-element compositions are consistent with a high-Si boninitic affinity, whereas the low-Cr# group (Cr#: 0.49−0.74) is interpreted to have experienced metasomatism by melts of adakitic affinity, as suggested by the occurrence of clinopyroxene with adakite-like geochemical characteristics. Compared to the mature Izu-Bonin and central Mariana arcs, the southwestern Mariana forearc peridotites are geochemically more enriched, showing higher SiO2 and Fe2O3T, lower MgO, and up to an order of magnitude higher heavy rare earth element contents. Our findings challenge the prevailing view that forearc peridotites are highly depleted and refractory and highlight the importance of early melt−rock interaction in shaping sub-arc mantle wedge compositions during subduction initiation.

item.title: Origin of negative Δ33S anomalies and Δ36S–Δ33S trends in 3700 Ma Isua sulfides: Searching for evidence of Eoarchean life in dolomitic meta-sedimentary rocks
item.dc:creator: Janaína N. Ávila
Allen P. Nutman
Vickie C. Bennett
Clark R.L. Friend
Martin Van Kranendonk
item.description: Proof of life in the oldest rock record is complex and at the limit of current scientific capabilities. However, investigations using the covariation between minor isotopes of sulfur (Δ33S and Δ36S) reveals the potential of using the sulfur isotope system to discriminate between biologically-influenced and entirely non-biological signatures. This paper reports new in-situ four sulfur isotope data measured on rare pyrite grains in Isua supracrustal belt (Greenland) 3700 Ma metamorphosed dolomitic rocks with primary sedimentary structures. The sulfide grains have negative Δ33S and positive Δ36S values indicative of photolytic sulfate as their primary sulfur source. The limited spread in δ34S values relative to an inferred Archean seawater sulfate reservoir, along with covariation between the minor sulfur isotopes (Δ33S and Δ36S) can be modelled by microbial sulfate reduction under conditions of low sulfate concentration (<100 μM). Potential abiogenic origins for these signatures are also considered, but they either fail to reproduce the observed sulfur isotope relationships or require reaction conditions or sulfur sources that are difficult to reconcile with the geological context of the Isua meta-dolomitic rocks. Our findings reinforce earlier interpretations that these pyrite-bearing dolomitic meta-sedimentary rocks have rare primary sedimentary structures preserved along with trace-element signatures indicative of biomediation in a marine-like environment. Collectively, these observations support microbial sulfate reduction as a coherent and geologically reasonable interpretation for the observed sulfur isotopic patterns when considered alongside the petrographic, sedimentological, and geochemical context. In this framework, the Isua sulfur isotope data would extend the documented record of microbial sulfate reduction by more than 200 million years, to ca. 3700 Ma, and provide one of the earliest geochemical constraints on the evolution of microbial sulfur metabolisms. Published: 2026-06-12T00:10:36+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.623 (consider)

Journal: Precambrian Research

DOI: 10.1016/j.precamres.2026.108188

Relevance: 0.623 (consider)

Proof of life in the oldest rock record is complex and at the limit of current scientific capabilities. However, investigations using the covariation between minor isotopes of sulfur (Δ33S and Δ36S) reveals the potential of using the sulfur isotope system to discriminate between biologically-influenced and entirely non-biological signatures. The limited spread in δ34S values relative to an inferred Archean seawater sulfate reservoir, along with covariation between the minor sulfur isotopes (Δ33S and Δ36S) can be modelled by microbial sulfate reduction under conditions of low sulfate concentration (<100 μM). Potential abiogenic origins for these signatures are also considered, but they either fail to reproduce the observed sulfur isotope relationships or require reaction conditions or sulfur sources that are difficult to reconcile with the geological context of the Isua meta-dolomitic rocks. Our findings reinforce earlier interpretations that these pyrite-bearing dolomitic meta-sedimentary rocks have rare primary sedimentary structures preserved along with trace-element signatures indicative of biomediation in a marine-like environment. Collectively, these observations support microbial sulfate reduction as a coherent and geologically reasonable interpretation for the observed sulfur isotopic patterns when considered alongside the petrographic, sedimentological, and geochemical context. In this framework, the Isua sulfur isotope data would extend the documented record of microbial sulfate reduction by more than 200 million years, to ca. 3700 Ma, and provide one of the earliest geochemical constraints on the evolution of microbial sulfur metabolisms.

item.title: Progressive crustal melting during low-angle subduction to slab rollback: insights from late Mesozoic granitoids in the Liaodong Peninsula
item.dc:creator: Jin-Feng Sun
Jin-Hui Yang
Ya-Dong Wu
Yu-Sheng Zhu
Lei Xu
Bao-Quan Zhou
Ji-Heng Zhang
Yan-Bin Zhang
item.description: Understanding how crustal melting responds to changes in subduction geometry is critical for reconstructing crustal evolution along convergent margins. In the eastern North China Craton, Late Mesozoic magmatism has been widely linked to the transition from low-angle subduction to rollback of the Paleo-Pacific plate, yet the geochemical response of crustal melting to this geodynamic shift remains incompletely constrained. Here we present new zircon UPb ages and HfO isotope data for nine Early Cretaceous granitic plutons from the Liaodong Peninsula and integrate them with previously published datasets. Four chronological stages are identified for the Late Mesozoic magmatism: Early−Middle Jurassic granitoids (182–165 Ma), Late Jurassic granitoids (159–155 Ma), Early Cretaceous syenitic rocks and A-type granites (129–123 Ma), and Early Cretaceous I-type granites (123–118 Ma). Jurassic granitoids were generated mainly by relatively high-pressure, low-temperature (ca. 756 °C), water-fluxed melting of lower crustal materials with garnet as a residual phase. Higher δ18O and strongly negative εHf(t) values of Late Jurassic granites indicate relatively more felsic source rocks than those of Early−Middle Jurassic granites. In contrast, the earliest Cretaceous syenitic rocks and A-type granites with variable La/Yb, Sr/Y ratios record high- temperature (ca. 822 °C), relatively dry crustal melting at deep and shallow crust levels. The subsequent Early Cretaceous I-type granites formed by relatively lower-temperature (ca. 756 °C) melting of a more hydrous lower crust, with involvement of mantle-derived materials. Together, these stages define a progressive reorganization of crustal melting conditions, i.e., from Jurassic high-pressure, low-temperature and water-rich to earliest Cretaceous decreasing pressure, high-temperature and water-absent, and finally to Early Cretaceous low-temperature and hydrous conditions. This temporal progression can be interpreted as a response to the transition from continental thickening to thinning, related to the shift from low-angle subduction to slab rollback of the Paleo-Pacific plate, highlighting how the petrogenetic diversity of granitoids can be used to trace crustal evolution in response to subduction dynamics. Published: 2026-06-14T16:39:36+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.621 (consider)

Authors: Jin-Feng Sun; Jin-Hui Yang; Ya-Dong Wu; Yu-Sheng Zhu; Lei Xu; Bao-Quan Zhou; Ji-Heng Zhang; Yan-Bin Zhang

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123565

Relevance: 0.621 (consider)

Understanding how crustal melting responds to changes in subduction geometry is critical for reconstructing crustal evolution along convergent margins. In the eastern North China Craton, Late Mesozoic magmatism has been widely linked to the transition from low-angle subduction to rollback of the Paleo-Pacific plate, yet the geochemical response of crustal melting to this geodynamic shift remains incompletely constrained. Here we present new zircon UPb ages and HfO isotope data for nine Early Cretaceous granitic plutons from the Liaodong Peninsula and integrate them with previously published datasets. Four chronological stages are identified for the Late Mesozoic magmatism: Early−Middle Jurassic granitoids (182–165 Ma), Late Jurassic granitoids (159–155 Ma), Early Cretaceous syenitic rocks and A-type granites (129–123 Ma), and Early Cretaceous I-type granites (123–118 Ma). Jurassic granitoids were generated mainly by relatively high-pressure, low-temperature (ca. 756 °C), water-fluxed melting of lower crustal materials with garnet as a residual phase. Higher δ18O and strongly negative εHf(t) values of Late Jurassic granites indicate relatively more felsic source rocks than those of Early−Middle Jurassic granites. In contrast, the earliest Cretaceous syenitic rocks and A-type granites with variable La/Yb, Sr/Y ratios record high- temperature (ca. 822 °C), relatively dry crustal melting at deep and shallow crust levels. The subsequent Early Cretaceous I-type granites formed by relatively lower-temperature (ca. 756 °C) melting of a more hydrous lower crust, with involvement of mantle-derived materials. Together, these stages define a progressive reorganization of crustal melting conditions, i.e., from Jurassic high-pressure, low-temperature and water-rich to earliest Cretaceous decreasing pressure, high-temperature and water-absent, and finally to Early Cretaceous low-temperature and hydrous conditions. This temporal progression can be interpreted as a response to the transition from continental thickening to thinning, related to the shift from low-angle subduction to slab rollback of the Paleo-Pacific plate, highlighting how the petrogenetic diversity of granitoids can be used to trace crustal evolution in response to subduction dynamics.

item.title: Neoproterozoic mafic complex in the Bikou microblock on the northwestern Yangtze Block: Implications for mantle source and tectonic evolution
item.dc:creator: Li Qin
Xianzhi Pei
Ruibao Li
Lei Pei
Chengjun Liu
Zuochen Li
Shaowei Zhao
Feng Gao
item.description: Neoproterozoic magmatic rocks exposed along the margins of the Yangtze Block provide key constraints for deciphering the tectonic evolution of the South China Plate and reconstructing its paleogeographic position within Rodinia. This study presents an integrated investigation of Neoproterozoic mafic complexes from the Bikou microblock in the northwestern Yangtze Block, using petrology, zircon U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, and zircon Lu-Hf isotopes to elucidate their petrogenesis and tectonic implications. Results show that the Yanzibian mafic complex formed at 905–902 Ma, whereas the Cangshe mafic complex formed at 882–881 Ma. The Yanzibian mafic complex is characterized by enrichments in light rare earth elements (LREEs) and large ion lithophile elements (LILEs), with relative depletions in Nb, Ta, P, and Ti, exhibiting typical geochemical affnities of arc magmas. Zircon ε Hf(t) values range from +8.84 to +12.92, with t DM1 ages of 1097–931 Ma. High Nb/Zr ratios and relatively constant Rb/Y ratios suggest that these magmas were derived from the partial melting of a mantle wedge metasomatized by subduction-related melts. The Cangshe mafic complex exhibits slight LREE enrichment without significant negative Nb, Ta, or Ti anomalies, resembling the geochemical characteristics of enriched mid-ocean ridge basalt (E-MORB). Zircon ε Hf(t) values vary from +8.66 to +13.34, with t DM1 ages of 1086–905 Ma. Moderate Nb/Yb and Th/Yb ratios indicate that their petrogenesis was related to enriched asthenospheric upwelling triggered by slab rollback. Combined with previous studies, our results further support a long-lived subduction-related tectonic setting for the northwestern Yangtze Block during the early Neoproterozoic, suggesting that it occupied a marginal position within Rodinia. Published: 2026-06-14T18:20:14+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.619 (consider)

Neoproterozoic magmatic rocks exposed along the margins of the Yangtze Block provide key constraints for deciphering the tectonic evolution of the South China Plate and reconstructing its paleogeographic position within Rodinia. This study presents an integrated investigation of Neoproterozoic mafic complexes from the Bikou microblock in the northwestern Yangtze Block, using petrology, zircon U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, and zircon Lu-Hf isotopes to elucidate their petrogenesis and tectonic implications. The Cangshe mafic complex exhibits slight LREE enrichment without significant negative Nb, Ta, or Ti anomalies, resembling the geochemical characteristics of enriched mid-ocean ridge basalt (E-MORB). Moderate Nb/Yb and Th/Yb ratios indicate that their petrogenesis was related to enriched asthenospheric upwelling triggered by slab rollback. Combined with previous studies, our results further support a long-lived subduction-related tectonic setting for the northwestern Yangtze Block during the early Neoproterozoic, suggesting that it occupied a marginal position within Rodinia.

item.title: Mesozoic continental crustal growth: A comprehensive review of the accretion-arc system in the Jilin-Heilongjiang high-pressure belt
item.dc:creator: Zhong-Hua Xin
Jian-Bo Zhou
Wen-Jiao Xiao
Jiang-Tao Han
Gong-Yu Li
Xiao-Miao Tan
Hong-Da Liang
Zhuo Chen
Hong-Yan Wang
Ning-Chen Sun
Rui Gao
item.description: The development of accretion-arc systems represents a fundamental mechanism of continental crustal growth and records key tectonic transitions during oceanic plate subduction. The Jilin-Heilongjiang High-Pressure Belt, eastern segment of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt, constitutes a classic accretion-arc system and provides an exceptional site for investigating geodynamic evolution. This review synthesizes geochronological, geochemical, deep seismic reflection, and magnetotelluric data, combined with other typical cases, to reveal the structural characteristics, formation mechanism, and continental growth geodynamic of the accretion-arc systems. Available geochronological constraints indicate that the protoliths of both the Zhangguangcai Complex and Heilongjiang Complex formed predominantly during the Permian and underwent successive metamorphism in the Early Jurassic, implying a unified tectonic regime linked to the evolution of the Heilongjiang Ocean. Arc-related magmatism displays typical subduction-related geochemical affinities, but is distinguished by relatively low 87Sr/86Sr ratios and high positive zircon εHf(t) values, reflecting substantial input from juvenile subduction-accretionary materials and marking the transition from accretionary growth to arc magmatism. Integrated seismic reflection and magnetotelluric imaging reveal imbricated, small-scale, short-arc reflectors with high resistivity beneath the accretionary complexes, underlain by mid-lower crustal high-conductivity, anti-U-shaped reflectors interpreted as deep-seated arc magmatic systems. These observations indicate that the Jilin-Heilongjiang High-Pressure Belt preserves a complete evolutionary sequence from accretionary wedge formation to arc magmatism. The fundamental geodynamic mechanism was dominated by prolonged low-angle westward subduction and subsequent plate rollback of the young Heilongjiang Ocean. Voluminous inputs of juvenile material underscore the critical role of accretion-arc systems in continental crustal growth. Published: 2026-06-13T00:54:36+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.614 (consider)

item.title: Neoarchean sanukitoids, metavolcanic rocks and TTGs of the northern Yangtze Craton, China: Insights into petrogenesis and crust-mantle interactions
item.dc:creator: Peiwen Liu
Xiaozhong Ding
Yanxue Liu
Tingdong Li
item.description: The northern part of the Yangtze Craton is one of the few regions in present-day South China with geological records dating back to the Archean. However, the geodynamic processes of Neoarchean continental crust formation and evolution in the northern Yangtze Craton remain poorly understood. In this study, we investigated four primary lithological assemblages from the Yudongzi Complex and Douling Complex in the northern Yangtze Craton and analyzed their petrogenesis. (1) The ~2.7 Ga Yudongzi meta-andesites exhibit typical characteristics of high-Mg andesites (HMAs) and originated from partial melting of a depleted mantle wedge metasomatized by fluids and sediment-derived melts. (2) The ~2.53 Ga Douling amphibolites display arc-type geochemical signatures and were derived from partial melting of a fluid-metasomatized depleted mantle wedge. (3) The ~2.5 Ga Douling tonalite–trondhjemite–granodiorite (TTG) gneisses are characterized by high Mg# values (45–57), elevated Cr (26–42 ppm) and Ni (19–31 ppm) concentrations, and positive zircon ɛHf(t) (+5.3 to +8.5) and whole-rock ɛNd(t) (−0.1 to +2.9) values. These features suggest that they formed via partial melting of a subducted oceanic slab. (4) The ~2.5 Ga Douling dioritic gneisses and quartz dioritic gneisses exhibit characteristics typical of Archean sanukitoids and formed by partial melting of a mantle wedge metasomatized by fluids and melts derived from sediments and oceanic slabs. These findings, together with the presence of contemporaneous subduction-related rocks in the northern Yangtze Craton, suggest that slab subduction was the dominant geodynamic process responsible for Neoarchean magmatism and continental crust formation in the region. Moreover, on the basis of thermodynamic and trace element simulations, the estimated partial melting P–T conditions for the slab-derived TTGs were approximately 1.2 GPa and 830 °C. This corresponds to geothermal gradients of ~19–22 °C/km, suggesting a warm and intermittent subduction environment during the Neoarchean in the northern Yangtze Craton. Published: 2026-06-12T00:02:37+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.613 (consider)

In this study, we investigated four primary lithological assemblages from the Yudongzi Complex and Douling Complex in the northern Yangtze Craton and analyzed their petrogenesis. (1) The ~2.7 Ga Yudongzi meta-andesites exhibit typical characteristics of high-Mg andesites (HMAs) and originated from partial melting of a depleted mantle wedge metasomatized by fluids and sediment-derived melts. (2) The ~2.53 Ga Douling amphibolites display arc-type geochemical signatures and were derived from partial melting of a fluid-metasomatized depleted mantle wedge. (3) The ~2.5 Ga Douling tonalite–trondhjemite–granodiorite (TTG) gneisses are characterized by high Mg# values (45–57), elevated Cr (26–42 ppm) and Ni (19–31 ppm) concentrations, and positive zircon ɛHf(t) (+5.3 to +8.5) and whole-rock ɛNd(t) (−0.1 to +2.9) values. (4) The ~2.5 Ga Douling dioritic gneisses and quartz dioritic gneisses exhibit characteristics typical of Archean sanukitoids and formed by partial melting of a mantle wedge metasomatized by fluids and melts derived from sediments and oceanic slabs. item.title: Lithium and calcium isotopes in various shallow-water carbonates: Disentangling the effects of mineralogy, vital effects, and early diagenesis
item.dc:creator: Guang-Yi Wei
Simon V. Hohl
Jack G. Murphy
Ashley N. Martin
Yi-Sheng Yin
Zhuang Xiao
Xiaobin Cao
Hong-Fei Ling
item.description: The isotopic composition of metals in marine carbonates provides a powerful archive of past seawater chemistry, yet these primary signals can be modified by diagenetic alteration and vital effects associated with biomineralization in biogenic carbonates. This study investigates the behavior of calcium (δ 44/40Ca) and lithium (δ7Li) isotopes across a diverse set of shallow-marine carbonate systems, including microbialites from Hamelin Pool in Shark Bay, Western Australia, and bulk carbonate sediments and biogenic corals from the Nansha Islands, South China Sea, to evaluate how depositional conditions, mineralogy, and early diagenesis govern their isotopic compositions. The microbialites exhibit low average values of δ 44/40Ca (–1.22 ± 0.23‰, 2σ, n = 8) and δ7Li (+21.1 ± 3.5‰, 2σ, n = 8), with their offsets from modern seawater values (δ 44/40CaSW = 0‰; δ7LiSW ≈ 31‰) primarily controlled by mineralogical composition. A positive correlation between δ 44/40Ca and δ7Li within different individual microbialites are also identified, which are not regulated by vital effects, salinity, or basin restriction, but likely reflect the modest diagenetic alteration of primary metastable aragonites. Compared with microbialites, bulk carbonate sediments show elevated average values of δ 44/40Ca (–1.09 ± 0.22‰, 2σ, n = 23) and δ7Li (+24.0 ± 1.3‰, 2σ, n = 30), indicating mixing of primary aragonite and high-Mg calcite in these sediments. In contrast, the shallow-water corals exhibit higher average δ 44/40Ca values (–1.02 ± 0.14‰, 2σ, n = 4) and lower δ7Li values (+18.0 ± 0.9‰, 2σ, n = 4), which we interpret as the influence of vital effects associated with isotopic fractionations during transmembrane transport of cations between seawater and the calcifying fluid for coral biomineralization. The agreement of δ 44/40Ca and δ7Li data from this study with published shallow-water carbonate records suggests that the isotopic compositions of primary bulk carbonate sediments are predominantly controlled by carbonate mineralogy, while additional processes, particularly vital effects in biogenic carbonates and early diagenetic alteration in carbonate sediments, can further influence these isotopic signatures. This underscores the need to account for carbonate mineralogy, vital effects, and diagenetic alteration when reconstructing coeval seawater signals from ancient carbonates, which can be done using multi-proxy isotope datasets and modeling framework. Further, when carbonate mineralogy and diagenesis is well constrained, microbialites may be used to reliably reconstruct δ 44/40Ca and δ7Li signatures of Precambrian seawater, as their isotopic signatures are largely insensitive to salinity, basin restriction, and vital effects Published: 2026-06-17T15:48:36+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.605 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Guang-Yi Wei; Simon V. Hohl; Jack G. Murphy; Ashley N. Martin; Yi-Sheng Yin; Zhuang Xiao; Xiaobin Cao; Hong-Fei Ling

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.06.022

Relevance: 0.605 (consider)

The isotopic composition of metals in marine carbonates provides a powerful archive of past seawater chemistry, yet these primary signals can be modified by diagenetic alteration and vital effects associated with biomineralization in biogenic carbonates. This study investigates the behavior of calcium (δ 44/40Ca) and lithium (δ7Li) isotopes across a diverse set of shallow-marine carbonate systems, including microbialites from Hamelin Pool in Shark Bay, Western Australia, and bulk carbonate sediments and biogenic corals from the Nansha Islands, South China Sea, to evaluate how depositional conditions, mineralogy, and early diagenesis govern their isotopic compositions. The microbialites exhibit low average values of δ 44/40Ca (–1.22 ± 0.23‰, 2σ, n = 8) and δ7Li (+21.1 ± 3.5‰, 2σ, n = 8), with their offsets from modern seawater values (δ 44/40CaSW = 0‰; δ7LiSW ≈ 31‰) primarily controlled by mineralogical composition. A positive correlation between δ 44/40Ca and δ7Li within different individual microbialites are also identified, which are not regulated by vital effects, salinity, or basin restriction, but likely reflect the modest diagenetic alteration of primary metastable aragonites. Compared with microbialites, bulk carbonate sediments show elevated average values of δ 44/40Ca (–1.09 ± 0.22‰, 2σ, n = 23) and δ7Li (+24.0 ± 1.3‰, 2σ, n = 30), indicating mixing of primary aragonite and high-Mg calcite in these sediments. In contrast, the shallow-water corals exhibit higher average δ 44/40Ca values (–1.02 ± 0.14‰, 2σ, n = 4) and lower δ7Li values (+18.0 ± 0.9‰, 2σ, n = 4), which we interpret as the influence of vital effects associated with isotopic fractionations during transmembrane transport of cations between seawater and the calcifying fluid for coral biomineralization. The agreement of δ 44/40Ca and δ7Li data from this study with published shallow-water carbonate records suggests that the isotopic compositions of primary bulk carbonate sediments are predominantly controlled by carbonate mineralogy, while additional processes, particularly vital effects in biogenic carbonates and early diagenetic alteration in carbonate sediments, can further influence these isotopic signatures. This underscores the need to account for carbonate mineralogy, vital effects, and diagenetic alteration when reconstructing coeval seawater signals from ancient carbonates, which can be done using multi-proxy isotope datasets and modeling framework. Further, when carbonate mineralogy and diagenesis is well constrained, microbialites may be used to reliably reconstruct δ 44/40Ca and δ7Li signatures of Precambrian seawater, as their isotopic signatures are largely insensitive to salinity, basin restriction, and vital effects

item.title: Quadruple sulfur and triple oxygen isotope constraints on biological processes in Mars-analog gypsum
item.dc:creator: Longye Du
Xiaoxiao Yu
Yiliang Li
Xiao Wu
Lin Gong
Gangjian Wei
Qiang Wang
Mang Lin
item.description: A primary objective of Mars sample return missions is the detection and characterization of potential biosignatures. Sulfates, among the most abundant hydrated minerals on Mars, represent promising targets for such investigation. Terrestrial studies have shown that gypsum can preserve life-associated organic matter and that its δ34S signatures provide key evidence for microbial sulfate reduction as early as ∼ 3.5 billion years ago. This study analyzes quadruple sulfur (δ34S, Δ33S, Δ36S) and triple oxygen (δ18O, Δ́17O) isotopes in gypsum from three strong evaporation basins on the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, where sulfate deposition is extensive but surface environments are only marginally habitable due to severe limitations in water, soil development, and nutrient availability. We focus on the Qaidam Basin as a Mars analog environment and place it in context through comparison with a higher-elevation region (Shuanghu) and a magmatic rock-dominated region (Yushu). Together with isotopic mixing models, an open-system steady-state sulfur cycle model, and triple-oxygen isotope oxidation pathway calculations, our quadruple sulfur and triple oxygen isotope measurements reveal that the studied gypsum deposits preserve distinct isotopic biosignatures. In particular, the isotopic compositions of the samples reflect microbial sulfur disproportionation and cryptic sulfur cycling, both of which extend beyond widespread microbial sulfate reduction, demonstrating how sulfur can be internally recycled and energetically utilized to sustain life in Mars-analog environments. This integrated isotopic approach significantly advances our capability to decipher sulfur cycling in extreme terrestrial environments, including Mars analog systems, and provides critical methodology for interpreting potential biosignatures in returned Martian samples. Published: 2026-06-18T16:26:21+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.602 (consider)

Authors: Longye Du; Xiaoxiao Yu; Yiliang Li; Xiao Wu; Lin Gong; Gangjian Wei; Qiang Wang; Mang Lin

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.06.019

Relevance: 0.602 (consider)

A primary objective of Mars sample return missions is the detection and characterization of potential biosignatures. Sulfates, among the most abundant hydrated minerals on Mars, represent promising targets for such investigation. Terrestrial studies have shown that gypsum can preserve life-associated organic matter and that its δ34S signatures provide key evidence for microbial sulfate reduction as early as ∼ 3.5 billion years ago. This study analyzes quadruple sulfur (δ34S, Δ33S, Δ36S) and triple oxygen (δ18O, Δ́17O) isotopes in gypsum from three strong evaporation basins on the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, where sulfate deposition is extensive but surface environments are only marginally habitable due to severe limitations in water, soil development, and nutrient availability. We focus on the Qaidam Basin as a Mars analog environment and place it in context through comparison with a higher-elevation region (Shuanghu) and a magmatic rock-dominated region (Yushu). Together with isotopic mixing models, an open-system steady-state sulfur cycle model, and triple-oxygen isotope oxidation pathway calculations, our quadruple sulfur and triple oxygen isotope measurements reveal that the studied gypsum deposits preserve distinct isotopic biosignatures. In particular, the isotopic compositions of the samples reflect microbial sulfur disproportionation and cryptic sulfur cycling, both of which extend beyond widespread microbial sulfate reduction, demonstrating how sulfur can be internally recycled and energetically utilized to sustain life in Mars-analog environments. This integrated isotopic approach significantly advances our capability to decipher sulfur cycling in extreme terrestrial environments, including Mars analog systems, and provides critical methodology for interpreting potential biosignatures in returned Martian samples.

item.title: Chemical mohometry, thick and thin: Critical review and new steps forward
item.dc:creator: Matthew J. Kohn
Allen F. Glazner
Chris Yakymchuk
Armando Lopez
Gloria Roman
item.description: Calculation of crustal thickness based on the geochemistry of arc magmas (chemical “mohometry”) relies on empirical correlations between seismically determined thickness and either elemental concentrations or ratios in modern arcs. Such calculations ignore the effects on magma chemistry of other intensive parameters, including oxygen fugacity (fO2), water fugacity (fH2O), and temperature, as well as crystallization history. Here, we review the global arc database to show that large data scatter requires averaging hundreds of measurements to converge on robust correlations, but that scatter is intrinsic to arc and volcano compositions such that increasing numbers of analyses cannot improve accuracy. Rather, uncertainties are uniform and range from ±17–19 km for entire arc segments to ±21–23 km for volcanic complexes. Strong chemical correlations among mohometers prevent reduction of this uncertainty. We further show that other factors besides crustal thickness, especially fO2, fH2O, T, and crystallization of major and accessory minerals, have major effects on trace-element chemistry of magmas. New and published trace element compositions for detrital zircon and whole-rocks from the east-central (Lhasa) sector of the Gangdese arc, southern Tibet, show that trends in trace element mohometers correlate with both T and fO2, rendering past estimates of crustal thickness and trends through time unreliable. Quantitative mohometry is currently no better than ~ ± 20 km, irrespective of number and quality of analyses, similar to random variation in arc crustal thickness (±25 km). Mohometry might be improved through analysis of detrital zircon, simultaneous characterization of fO2 and T, development of a geochemical proxy for fH2O, and further modeling. Published: 2026-06-14T16:32:25+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.599 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Matthew J. Kohn; Allen F. Glazner; Chris Yakymchuk; Armando Lopez; Gloria Roman

Journal: Earth-Science Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.earscirev.2026.105583

Relevance: 0.599 (consider)

Calculation of crustal thickness based on the geochemistry of arc magmas (chemical “mohometry”) relies on empirical correlations between seismically determined thickness and either elemental concentrations or ratios in modern arcs. Such calculations ignore the effects on magma chemistry of other intensive parameters, including oxygen fugacity (fO2), water fugacity (fH2O), and temperature, as well as crystallization history. Here, we review the global arc database to show that large data scatter requires averaging hundreds of measurements to converge on robust correlations, but that scatter is intrinsic to arc and volcano compositions such that increasing numbers of analyses cannot improve accuracy. Rather, uncertainties are uniform and range from ±17–19 km for entire arc segments to ±21–23 km for volcanic complexes. Strong chemical correlations among mohometers prevent reduction of this uncertainty. We further show that other factors besides crustal thickness, especially fO2, fH2O, T, and crystallization of major and accessory minerals, have major effects on trace-element chemistry of magmas. New and published trace element compositions for detrital zircon and whole-rocks from the east-central (Lhasa) sector of the Gangdese arc, southern Tibet, show that trends in trace element mohometers correlate with both T and fO2, rendering past estimates of crustal thickness and trends through time unreliable. Quantitative mohometry is currently no better than ~ ± 20 km, irrespective of number and quality of analyses, similar to random variation in arc crustal thickness (±25 km). Mohometry might be improved through analysis of detrital zircon, simultaneous characterization of fO2 and T, development of a geochemical proxy for fH2O, and further modeling.

item.title: Metallogenic age, material sources, and ore-forming process of the Donggou VMS-type zinc deposit in the Qimantag Area, East Kunlun Orogenic Belt
item.dc:creator: Haiming Guo
Xiaoyong Yang
Huishan Zhang
Guowen Miao
Bin Wang
Yu Yang
Yiming Chen
Yunping Li
Fukun Chen
item.description: The Donggou zinc deposit is the first discovered volcanogenic massive sulfide (VMS)-type lead–zinc deposit in the East Kunlun orogen, holding significant scientific value and economic potential. As a newly identified deposit type in the region, key scientific issues such as its metallogenic age, sources of ore-forming materials, ore-forming conditions, and genetic mechanism remain unclear. This study systematically analyzes the major and trace element compositions of sphalerite from different ore-forming stages within the main mineralization period, the in situ S-Pb isotopic characteristics of major metallic sulfides, and zircon U-Pb dating of the host rocks. The aim is to delineate the ore-forming process and elucidate the genetic mechanism of the deposit. Results indicate that the main mineralization period of the Donggou deposit can be divided into three stages: stockwork stage (I), massive sulfide stage (II), and laminated stage (III). The trace element compositions of sphalerite from these stages (Sp1, Sp2, Sp3) show significant differences: Sp1 is relatively enriched in Cu, In, Se, and Co; Sp2 is enriched in Mn, Ag, As, and Pb; and Sp3 is characterized by enrichment in Sb and Ga. Temperature estimates based on the GGIMFIS geothermometer for sphalerite are 314-361°C (avg. 334°C) for Sp1, 296-336°C (avg. 317°C) for Sp2, and 286-334°C (avg. 308°C) for Sp3. Corresponding sulfur fugacity (lg f s 2 ) ranges are −6.79 to −4.93 for Sp1, −7.60 to −5.89 for Sp2, and −8.10 to −6.23 for Sp3. These indicate that the ore-forming fluids were generated under medium‑ to high‑temperature and relatively high sulfur fugacity conditions. From early to late mineralization, temperature remained generally stable, while sulfur fugacity gradually decreased. In situ δ34S values of sulfides (−3.59‰ to 6.24‰) suggest that sulfur was primarily derived from the degassing of a shallow magma chamber or the leaching of wall rocks, with minor incorporation of seawater sulfate sulfur formed via thermochemical sulfate reduction (TSR). Lead isotope compositions of galena (208Pb/204Pb = 37.980–38.021, 207Pb/204Pb = 15.575–15.613, 206Pb/204Pb = 18.210–18.261) display characteristics of a mixture derived from basement and wall rocks. Zircon U-Pb dating of the host rock yields an age of 446 ± 1.5 Ma, indicating that the deposit formed during the Late Ordovician subduction of the Qimantag Ocean. In summary, this study delineates for the first time the metallogenic age, material sources, and evolution of physicochemical conditions of the ore-forming fluids for the Donggou VMS-type zinc deposit, providing a detailed reconstruction of the ore-forming process. These findings are significant for understanding VMS-type mineralization in the East Kunlun orogen and for guiding regional mineral resources exploration. Published: 2026-06-17T23:34:28+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.596 (consider)

Corresponding sulfur fugacity (lg f s 2 ) ranges are −6.79 to −4.93 for Sp1, −7.60 to −5.89 for Sp2, and −8.10 to −6.23 for Sp3. item.title: Genetic relationships of two-stage Rb-enriched granite intrusions: Evidence from Changlingjian granites in the Jiangnan tungsten belt, southern China
item.dc:creator: Fei Zhang
Dayu Zhang
Mengxiang Hu
Hanghang Lei
Wangfei Weng
Taofa Zhou
Noel C. White
item.description: It has been debated for decades whether Rb-rich granitic melts were formed by fractional crystallization or source enrichment. The factors controlling the enrichment of different Rb-rich granites are also unclear. Two types of Rb-rich granitic intrusions, i.e., muscovite granite and granite porphyry, occur in the Changlingjian W deposit, Southern China, providing an opportunity to clarify the Rb enrichment mechanism in granitic melts. We systematically compared the petrographic, geochronological, and geochemical characteristics of the muscovite granite and granite porphyry intrusions. The results showed that muscovite granite (131.4 ± 1.9 Ma) is characterized as a potassic calc-alkaline peraluminous S-type granite, which is approximately 10 Ma older than the granite porphyry (122.7 ± 1.8 Ma). Geochemistry and simulation results showed that the muscovite granite and granite porphyry melts were sequential partial melting products derived from the same Shuangqiaoshan Group metapelite. The muscovite granite melt was formed by low-temperature (~ 700 °C) partial melting, whereas the granitic porphyry melt was generated from residual magmatic sources by high-temperature (> 800 °C) partial remelting. Quantitative partial melting and fractional crystallization modeling suggests that the magmatic source significantly affected the formation of Rb-rich granitic melts. Published: 2026-06-15T23:27:23+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.595 (consider)

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108680

Relevance: 0.595 (consider)

It has been debated for decades whether Rb-rich granitic melts were formed by fractional crystallization or source enrichment. The factors controlling the enrichment of different Rb-rich granites are also unclear. Two types of Rb-rich granitic intrusions, i.e., muscovite granite and granite porphyry, occur in the Changlingjian W deposit, Southern China, providing an opportunity to clarify the Rb enrichment mechanism in granitic melts. We systematically compared the petrographic, geochronological, and geochemical characteristics of the muscovite granite and granite porphyry intrusions. The results showed that muscovite granite (131.4 ± 1.9 Ma) is characterized as a potassic calc-alkaline peraluminous S-type granite, which is approximately 10 Ma older than the granite porphyry (122.7 ± 1.8 Ma). Geochemistry and simulation results showed that the muscovite granite and granite porphyry melts were sequential partial melting products derived from the same Shuangqiaoshan Group metapelite. The muscovite granite melt was formed by low-temperature (~ 700 °C) partial melting, whereas the granitic porphyry melt was generated from residual magmatic sources by high-temperature (> 800 °C) partial remelting. Quantitative partial melting and fractional crystallization modeling suggests that the magmatic source significantly affected the formation of Rb-rich granitic melts.

item.title: Punctuated energetics of dehydration contributes to slow earthquake phenomena
item.dc:creator: Timothy Chapman
Sandra Piazolo
Geoffrey Clarke
Lars Hansen
Manon Carpenter
item.description: The prograde breakdown of hydrous minerals like lawsonite is energy-intensive and releases fluids at depths coincident with extensive slow earthquake phenomena for many subduction geotherms. However, there remains inconsistency between the spatial occurrence and inferred temporal rates of these dehydration reactions relative to the episodic character of slow earthquakes. The example of lawsonite breakdown shows that dehydration reactions will dictate heat consumption, thus buffering the rate of temperature increase for a rock undergoing intervals of metamorphism (<30 °C) and delaying each small increment (∼1 °C) of fluid release. A nonlinear behavior is predicted involving periods of fast fluid release paused by periods of slower heat absorption. Such stepped reaction progress is consistent with natural rock textures of lawsonite breakdown indicating intermittent fluid-present crack propagation and associated step changes in mineral growth. Pulsed fluid flow into rocks of heterogeneous permeability may result in stress build-up enabling crack propagation and slip at different scales. Energetics of punctuated dehydration and resultant fluid flow match temporal and spatial characteristics of slow slip behavior. Consequently, episodic dehydration is suggested as a viable trigger for slow slip. Published: 2026-06-18T18:52:10+00:00 Venue: Geology Score: 0.593 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Timothy Chapman; Sandra Piazolo; Geoffrey Clarke; Lars Hansen; Manon Carpenter

Journal: Geology

DOI: 10.1130/g54149.1

Relevance: 0.593 (consider)

The prograde breakdown of hydrous minerals like lawsonite is energy-intensive and releases fluids at depths coincident with extensive slow earthquake phenomena for many subduction geotherms. However, there remains inconsistency between the spatial occurrence and inferred temporal rates of these dehydration reactions relative to the episodic character of slow earthquakes. The example of lawsonite breakdown shows that dehydration reactions will dictate heat consumption, thus buffering the rate of temperature increase for a rock undergoing intervals of metamorphism (<30 °C) and delaying each small increment (∼1 °C) of fluid release. A nonlinear behavior is predicted involving periods of fast fluid release paused by periods of slower heat absorption. Such stepped reaction progress is consistent with natural rock textures of lawsonite breakdown indicating intermittent fluid-present crack propagation and associated step changes in mineral growth. Pulsed fluid flow into rocks of heterogeneous permeability may result in stress build-up enabling crack propagation and slip at different scales. Energetics of punctuated dehydration and resultant fluid flow match temporal and spatial characteristics of slow slip behavior. Consequently, episodic dehydration is suggested as a viable trigger for slow slip.

item.title: Consequences of the East and West Gondwana collision revealed in the northern Arabian-Nubian Shield of Egypt: New insights from the pre-, late-, and post-collision granitoids
item.dc:creator: Osama K. Dessouky
Yuruo Shi
Hani H. Ali
Kamal A. Ali
Sobhi Nassir
Ahmed M. Dardier
Mohammed Z. El-Bialy
A. Abu-Deif
Simon A. Wilde
Mahmoud A. Hassan
item.description: The Arabian-Nubian Shield (ANS) is one of Earth’s largest tracts of juvenile continental crust, preserving key evidence of the transition from pre-, to late-, to post-collision magmatism during the Neoproterozoic assembly of Gondwana. To decipher these changes, we conducted an integrated study of the El-Missikat–El-Erediya granitic suite in the Egyptian Eastern Desert, combining SHRIMP zircon U–Pb geochronology, Hf isotopes, whole-rock geochemistry, and fluorine analyses. Pre-collision arc granitoids dated at 672 ± 20 Ma are calc-alkaline with I-type signatures, typical of subduction-related magmatism. In contrast, late-collision syenogranites crystallized between 610 ± 11 and 597 ± 12 Ma and have A2-type affinities, with high SiO2, pronounced negative Eu anomalies, and enrichment in HFSE and REE. Their geochemical features include advanced fractionation in the presence of fluorine-rich fluids. Zircon εHf(t) values of the arc granitoids (+8 to +12) indicate derivation from a juvenile depleted mantle source (HftDM model ages of ∼ 900–743 Ma), whereas the late-collisional granites show more variable compositions (εHf(t) = +5 to +13; and HftDM model ages of ∼980–669 Ma), reflecting mixing of mantle and reworked crustal contributions. Geochemical data, including elevated Nb/Y and Ta/Yb ratios, imply asthenospheric upwelling was triggered by slab break-off and facilitated partial melting of the lithosphere and lower crust, signifying the tectonic transition from compression to extension during the final stages of Gondwana amalgamation. This framework advances our understanding of late-orogenic processes in accretionary terranes by linking slab break-off to the generation of late-collisional A2-type granites (>600 Ma) within a thickened orogen. Furthermore, it reflects the collision of East and West Gondwana and defines the initial stabilization of the supercontinent, distinguishing this process from later orogenic collapse driven by lithospheric delamination, which produced post-collision A1-type granites at <600 Ma. Published: 2026-06-14T17:45:22+00:00 Venue: Gondwana Research Score: 0.589 (consider)
